old_claim.note: Interchangeable parts scaled the replacement-part and specification use case for industrial catalogs, but trade catalogs could exist for non-interchangeable goods.

new_claim_absent: Industrial trade catalogs existed before Hall's 1822 interchangeable-parts milestone, so later replacement-part demand is retained in scope notes rather than modeled as an origin edge.

source_shape.source_locator: Smithsonian defines trade literature as printed or handwritten lists of items for sale and says manufacturers issued trade catalogs to promote and sell products.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Smithsonian definition covers manufacturer catalogs and sales lists without limiting them to standardized interchangeable replacement parts.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Printing and merchandising explain the early catalog form directly; interchangeability is a later product characteristic that expands one catalog use case but reverses first-known chronology.

ontology_before: Industrial parts catalogs retained a later interchangeable-parts scaling edge even though the node includes earlier general industrial trade literature.
ontology_after: Industrial parts catalogs inherit from printing, while later standardized replacement-part cataloging remains a maturity use case rather than an origin dependency.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: industrial_parts_catalogs no longer directly depends on interchangeable_parts.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to catalogs exclusively listing standardized interchangeable replacement parts first documented after 1822.
short_reason: General industrial trade catalogs predate interchangeable replacement parts.
